title: NIST 800-171 CUI Protection Requirements
content:
# NIST 800-171 CUI Protection Requirements

## Overview

NIST Special Publication 800-171 Revision 2 defines the security requirements for protecting Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) in nonfederal systems and organizations. It is the foundational standard for CMMC Level 2, DFARS 252.204-7012 compliance, and is increasingly referenced in federal contracts beyond the DoD.

CUI encompasses a broad range of government information that requires safeguarding, including technical data, export-controlled information, law enforcement sensitive data, and certain financial and privacy information. The CUI Registry (https://www.archives.gov/cui) defines all CUI categories and subcategories.

SOC teams at organizations handling CUI must ensure their security operations satisfy the 110 security requirements across 14 families.

## The 14 Security Requirement Families

| Family | ID | Requirements | Description |
|--------|----|-------------|-------------|
| Access Control | 3.1 | 22 | Limit system access, enforce least privilege, control remote access |
| Awareness and Training | 3.2 | 3 | Security awareness, training for roles, insider threat awareness |
| Audit and Accountability | 3.3 | 9 | Create, protect, and review audit records |
| Configuration Management | 3.4 | 9 | Establish baselines, enforce security settings, control changes |
| Identification and Authentication | 3.5 | 11 | Identify and authenticate users, devices, and processes |
| Incident Response | 3.6 | 3 | Establish IR capability, track and report incidents |
| Maintenance | 3.7 | 6 | Perform and log system maintenance |
| Media Protection | 3.8 | 9 | Protect, sanitize, and control system media |
| Personnel Security | 3.9 | 2 | Screen individuals, protect CUI during personnel actions |
| Physical Protection | 3.10 | 6 | Limit physical access, protect and monitor facility |
| Risk Assessment | 3.11 | 3 | Assess risk, scan for vulnerabilities, remediate |
| Security Assessment | 3.12 | 4 | Assess controls, develop POA&M, monitor continuously |
| System and Communications Protection | 3.13 | 16 | Monitor boundaries, implement cryptographic protections |
| System and Information Integrity | 3.14 | 7 | Identify flaws, monitor systems, detect malicious code |

## Detailed Requirements by Family

### 3.1 Access Control (22 Requirements)

**Critical Requirements**:

| Requirement | Description | Implementation Guidance |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 3.1.1 | Limit system access to authorized users | Implement centralized IAM, disable default accounts, enforce authentication |
| 3.1.2 | Limit system access to permitted transaction types | Role-based access control, function-level authorization |
| 3.1.3 | Control the flow of CUI in accordance with approved authorizations | Data flow controls, DLP, network segmentation |
| 3.1.5 | Employ the principle of least privilege | Minimum necessary access, regular access reviews |
| 3.1.6 | Use non-privileged accounts for non-security functions | Separate admin and standard user accounts |
| 3.1.7 | Prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions | UAC enforcement, sudo logging, privilege escalation prevention |
| 3.1.12 | Monitor and control remote access sessions | VPN with MFA, session recording for CUI systems, timeout policies |
| 3.1.13 | Employ cryptographic mechanisms to protect confidentiality of remote access sessions | FIPS-validated encryption for all remote access (TLS 1.2+, IPsec) |
| 3.1.14 | Route remote access via managed access control points | All remote access through VPN concentrator or zero-trust gateway |
| 3.1.20 | Verify and control/limit connections to external systems | Firewall rules, connection whitelisting, external system authorization |
| 3.1.22 | Control CUI posted on publicly accessible systems | Pre-publication review, automated scanning for CUI markers |

### 3.3 Audit and Accountability (9 Requirements)

| Requirement | Description | Implementation Guidance |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 3.3.1 | Create and retain system audit logs | SIEM collection from all CUI systems, minimum 1-year retention |
| 3.3.2 | Ensure individual accountability for audited events | Unique user IDs, no shared accounts, log user identity in all events |
| 3.3.3 | Review and update logged events | Annual review of audit event selection, update based on incidents |
| 3.3.4 | Alert on audit logging process failure | Monitor SIEM health, alert on log source dropoff, verify collection |
| 3.3.5 | Correlate audit record review, analysis, and reporting | SIEM correlation rules, automated analysis, scheduled reporting |
| 3.3.6 | Provide audit record reduction and report generation | Dashboard views, automated compliance reports, trend analysis |
| 3.3.7 | Provide system clock synchronization | NTP configuration, time source verification, drift alerting |
| 3.3.8 | Protect audit information and tools | Read-only SIEM access for analysts, admin access restricted |
| 3.3.9 | Limit management of audit logging to a subset of privileged users | Separate audit admin role, dual control for log deletion |

### 3.5 Identification and Authentication (11 Requirements)

| Requirement | Description | Implementation Guidance |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 3.5.1 | Identify system users, processes, and devices | Unique identifiers for all entities, device certificates |
| 3.5.2 | Authenticate identities as prerequisite to access | Authentication before any system interaction |
| 3.5.3 | Use multifactor authentication for local and network access to privileged accounts | MFA required: something you know + something you have/are |
| 3.5.4 | Employ replay-resistant authentication for network access to privileged accounts | Token-based auth, Kerberos, FIDO2 (not SMS) |
| 3.5.5 | Prevent reuse of identifiers | Unique IDs never reassigned, deactivated IDs retained |
| 3.5.6 | Disable identifiers after period of inactivity | 90-day inactivity timeout, automated disable |
| 3.5.7 | Enforce minimum password complexity and change of characters | 12+ chars, complexity requirements, dictionary check |
| 3.5.8 | Prohibit password reuse for specified number of generations | Minimum 24-generation password history |
| 3.5.9 | Allow temporary password use with immediate change | Force password change on first use |
| 3.5.10 | Store and transmit only cryptographically-protected passwords | Bcrypt/scrypt hashing, no plaintext transmission |
| 3.5.11 | Obscure feedback of authentication information | Mask password fields, no password display in logs |

### 3.6 Incident Response (3 Requirements)

**3.6.1 - Establish an operational incident-handling capability**

Required elements:
- Preparation: tools, training, communication plans, CUI-specific procedures
- Detection and Analysis: monitoring, alert triage, CUI impact assessment
- Containment: pre-authorized containment actions, CUI isolation procedures
- Eradication and Recovery: malware removal, system rebuild, CUI verification
- Post-Incident: lessons learned, control updates, reporting

**3.6.2 - Track, document, and report incidents**

- Case management system for all incidents involving CUI
- DFARS 252.204-7012 reporting: within 72 hours to DIBNet
- Preserve forensic evidence for minimum 90 days
- Provide forensic images to DC3 upon request

**3.6.3 - Test the organizational incident response capability**

- Annual tabletop exercises (minimum)
- Scenario must include CUI compromise scenarios
- Document results and update IR plan based on findings
- Track remediation items from exercises

### 3.13 System and Communications Protection (16 Requirements)

| Requirement | Description | Implementation Guidance |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 3.13.1 | Monitor, control, and protect communications at external boundaries | Firewall, IDS/IPS, proxy at all CUI network boundaries |
| 3.13.2 | Employ architectural designs that promote effective security | Defense in depth, network segmentation, DMZ |
| 3.13.5 | Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible components | DMZ for public-facing systems, no direct CUI access |
| 3.13.6 | Deny network traffic by default; allow by exception | Default-deny firewall rules, documented allow rules |
| 3.13.8 | Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure during transmission | FIPS 140-2 validated encryption, TLS 1.2 minimum |
| 3.13.11 | Employ FIPS-validated cryptography | All encryption modules must be FIPS 140-2 or 140-3 validated |
| 3.13.16 | Protect CUI at rest | Full-disk encryption or file-level encryption with FIPS modules |

### 3.14 System and Information Integrity (7 Requirements)

| Requirement | Description | Implementation Guidance |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 3.14.1 | Identify, report, and correct system flaws in a timely manner | Vulnerability management program, patching SLAs |
| 3.14.2 | Provide malicious code protection | EDR/antimalware on all CUI endpoints and servers |
| 3.14.3 | Monitor security alerts and advisories and take action | Subscribe to vendor advisories, US-CERT, CISA alerts |
| 3.14.4 | Update malicious code protection mechanisms | Automated signature updates, verify currency daily |
| 3.14.5 | Perform periodic and real-time scans | Monthly vulnerability scans, real-time malware scanning |
| 3.14.6 | Monitor systems including inbound and outbound traffic | Network and endpoint monitoring, DLP for CUI |
| 3.14.7 | Identify unauthorized use of systems | User behavior monitoring, anomaly detection for CUI access |

## CUI Boundary Definition

Defining the CUI boundary is critical for scoping security requirements:

### Step 1: Identify CUI Data
- Review contracts for CUI marking requirements
- Identify CUI categories from the CUI Registry
- Catalog all CUI received from government agencies
- Identify CUI generated internally (derivative CUI)

### Step 2: Map CUI Data Flows
- Document systems that process, store, or transmit CUI
- Map data flows between systems (internal and external)
- Identify all users who access CUI
- Document third parties who receive CUI

### Step 3: Define the Assessment Boundary
- All systems in the CUI data flow are in scope
- Security infrastructure protecting CUI systems is in scope
- Shared services (DNS, DHCP, AD) used by CUI systems are in scope
- Document out-of-scope systems with justification

### Step 4: Consider Boundary Reduction
- Network segmentation to isolate CUI systems
- Enclave approach: dedicated CUI processing environment
- Cloud-based CUI enclave with FedRAMP-authorized CSP
- Virtual desktop infrastructure (VDI) for CUI access

## NIST 800-171A Assessment Procedures

NIST SP 800-171A provides assessment procedures for each requirement:

| Assessment Method | Description | Usage |
|------------------|-------------|-------|
| Examine | Review documents, mechanisms, activities | Policy review, configuration screenshots, log samples |
| Interview | Discuss with personnel | Verify understanding of procedures, validate process |
| Test | Exercise mechanisms and activities | Validate technical controls function correctly |

## Scoring Methodology (NIST SP 800-171 DoD Assessment)

DoD uses a scoring methodology for 800-171 assessments:

- **110 points maximum** (one point per requirement)
- **Basic Assessment**: Self-assessment with medium confidence
- **Medium Assessment**: Self-assessment with government review, medium confidence
- **High Assessment**: Government-conducted assessment, high confidence

Deductions are weighted by requirement importance:

| Weight | Impact | Example Requirements |
|--------|--------|---------------------|
| 5 points | Critical | MFA (3.5.3), FIPS encryption (3.13.11), audit logging (3.3.1) |
| 3 points | High | Access control (3.1.1), incident response (3.6.1), monitoring (3.14.6) |
| 1 point | Standard | Training (3.2.1), maintenance (3.7.1), physical (3.10.1) |

Score is reported in the Supplier Performance Risk System (SPRS).

## Common Compliance Gaps

1. **FIPS-validated encryption not implemented** - Using standard TLS/AES without verifying FIPS validation. Check cryptographic module certificates against the CMVP validated modules list.

2. **Insufficient CUI scoping** - CUI exists on more systems than documented. Conduct data discovery including email, SharePoint, and endpoint file systems.

3. **Missing MFA for all CUI access** - MFA only on VPN but not on individual CUI applications. Implement MFA at the application layer for all CUI systems.

4. **Audit log gaps** - Not all CUI systems forwarding logs to SIEM. Audit log source inventory against CUI system inventory quarterly.

5. **No CUI-specific incident response procedures** - Generic IR plan without CUI handling specifics. Add CUI-specific runbooks including 72-hour DoD reporting.

6. **Shared accounts on CUI systems** - Service accounts or shared admin credentials. Implement individual accountability for all CUI access.

## References

- NIST SP 800-171 Rev 2: https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-171/rev-2/final
- NIST SP 800-171A (Assessment Procedures): https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-171a/final
- NIST SP 800-171 Rev 3 (Draft): https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-171/rev-3/final
- CUI Registry: https://www.archives.gov/cui
- DFARS 252.204-7012: Safeguarding Covered Defense Information
- SPRS: https://www.sprs.csd.disa.mil/
